one-to-many-and-many-to-many-results.label: One-to-Many and Many-to-Many Results
one-to-many-and-many-to-many-results.summary: Predict why one parent row may appear several times after joining to related detail rows.

try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.title: Join Departments and Courses
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.prompt: Write a query to list each course along with its department name. Use the `departments` and `courses` tables.
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.hint: Ask what one output row represents.

help.concept: Relationship shape explains why a parent may repeat and what one joined result row represents.
help.hint_1: Identify the detail or bridge row that can repeat for one parent.
help.hint_2: State the result grain in plain language before deciding whether repeated rows are correct.

try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.title: List Students and Their Courses
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.prompt: Write a query to list each student along with the courses they are enrolled in. Use the `students`, `enrollments`, and `courses` tables.
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.hint: Ask what one output row represents.

help.concept: Relationship shape explains why a parent may repeat and what one joined result row represents.
help.hint_1: Identify the detail or bridge row that can repeat for one parent.
help.hint_2: State the result grain in plain language before deciding whether repeated rows are correct.

try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.starterCode:
SELECT 
FROM students
JOIN enrollments ON 
JOIN courses ON ;
try_one_to_many_and_many_to_many_results.solutionCode:
SELECT students.name AS student_name, courses.title AS course_title
FROM students
JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id;

fill-blank-choice-1.title: SQL Join Syntax
fill-blank-choice-1.prompt: Complete the SQL statement to join `students` and `enrollments` on the student ID.
fill-blank-choice-1.hint: Use the correct SQL syntax for joining tables.

help.concept: The `ON` clause specifies the condition for joining tables.
help.hint_1: The `ON` clause should match the primary key of one table to the foreign key of another.
help.hint_2: Ensure the column names are correctly qualified with table names.

fill-blank-choice-2.title: Name the Result Grain
fill-blank-choice-2.prompt: After joining departments to courses without grouping, what does one result row represent?
fill-blank-choice-2.hint: Read the selected detail table and identify which row can repeat for one department.

help.concept: Result grain describes what one output row represents; it is not determined by a relationship label alone.
help.hint_1: A department can own several course rows.
help.hint_2: The ungrouped joined result has one row for each matching course.

fill-blank-choice-2.template: One result row represents [blank1] together with its department.

multi-choice-1.title: Characteristics of Many-to-Many Relationships
multi-choice-1.prompt: Select all characteristics of a many-to-many relationship.
multi-choice-1.hint: Consider how many-to-many relationships are structured.

help.concept: Many-to-many relationships involve a bridge table to connect two tables.
help.hint_1: Think about the role of the bridge table.
help.hint_2: Consider how each table can relate to multiple records in the other.

options.b: Each record in one table can relate to multiple records in the other
options.c: Directly connects two tables without intermediaries

drag-reorder-1.title: Order of SQL Join Operations
drag-reorder-1.prompt: Arrange the steps to perform a join between `students` and `courses` using `enrollments`.
drag-reorder-1.hint: Think about the sequence of joining tables in a many-to-many relationship.

help.concept: Joining tables in a many-to-many relationship involves using a bridge table.
help.hint_1: Start by joining the first table to the bridge table.
help.hint_2: Then join the bridge table to the second table.

tokens.t3: Select columns from students and courses

sketch0.title: Understanding One-to-Many Relationships
sketch0.bodyMarkdown:
In a one-to-many relationship, a single record in one table can be associated with multiple records in another table. For example, consider the relationship between `departments` and `courses`. Each department can offer multiple courses, but each course belongs to only one department. 

```sql
SELECT departments.name AS department_name, courses.title AS course_title
FROM departments
JOIN courses ON departments.id = courses.department_id;
```

This query joins the `departments` table with the `courses` table, showing each course along with its department. Notice how a department like 'Science' can appear multiple times, once for each course it offers.

sketch1.title: Exploring Many-to-Many Relationships
sketch1.bodyMarkdown:
Many-to-many relationships involve a bridge table to connect two tables. For instance, students can enroll in multiple courses, and each course can have multiple students. This is managed through the `enrollments` table, which acts as a bridge between `students` and `courses`.

```sql
SELECT students.name AS student_name, courses.title AS course_title
FROM students
JOIN enrollments ON students.id = enrollments.student_id
JOIN courses ON enrollments.course_id = courses.id;
```

This query retrieves the names of students along with the courses they are enrolled in. The `enrollments` table allows us to connect `students` and `courses`, enabling a many-to-many relationship.
